content.assessment: The status-quo counterfactual perpetuates and likely deepens existing distributional inequities because survival of village schools depends on differential local social capital and district discretion rather than transparent, rule-based criteria. Given the already severe urban-rural outcome gap, unequal early school leaving rates, and the documented drift of segregated village schools toward church maintainers without addressing their more advantaged intake, this pathway offers no correction to sorting or access mechanisms and instead relies on informal, unevenly distributed political leverage.
opportunities: Could still be paired with future statutory reform without foreclosing options, since no binding structural commitments are made
strengths: Preserves current access levels for villages that happen to have strong local mobilization capacity, avoiding abrupt closures in those cases (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)
threats: Continued territorial concentration of disadvantage risks entrenching ghetto schools concentrated in North-East Hungary as enrollment decline continues (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)
Unequal baseline future options (early leaving rates for Roma vs non-Roma) are reinforced rather than mitigated absent any corrective mechanism (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)
weaknesses: Survival determined by informal social capital rather than demographic or equity criteria produces arbitrary distributional outcomes across similarly-situated villages

No mechanism addresses the contested pattern of segregated schools being handed to church maintainers, which serve more advantaged intakes and may let government evade responsibility for segregation (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
Teacher shortages and aging workforce concentrated in rural/disadvantaged schools remain unaddressed by ad hoc administrative practice (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09)
Rural-urban outcome gap, already the largest among OECD countries, has no structural remedy under continued informal negotiation (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
